title: Define responsive breakpoints before layout
summary: AI-generated interfaces often lack responsive breakpoints, producing single-viewport layouts that break on mobile or stretch endlessly on wide screens.
instruction: Define at least one narrow-viewport breakpoint before implementing layout. Test every major layout section at the widest and narrowest target viewports. Use fluid typography and relative units.
rationale: A layout that only works at the AI's default viewport width (typically 1280-1440px) will overflow, stack poorly, or become unreadable on mobile devices. Responsive design is not optional for web interfaces.
violations: A marketing page with fixed-width containers (1200px), font sizes in px, and no media queries that overflows horizontally on mobile.
An article layout with fixed sidebar (300px) and content (800px) that forces horizontal scrolling on viewports under 1100px.
controls: A marketing page using max-width: 1200px with width: 100% on containers, fluid type (clamp()), and three breakpoints (mobile 375px, tablet 768px, desktop 1024px+).
An editorial layout using a single-column stack on mobile and two-column grid on desktop via CSS Grid auto-fit.
borderlineCases: A dashboard designed for a specific kiosk display with a known fixed resolution may intentionally omit responsive breakpoints, but should document this.
contextualExceptions: Internal tool accessed only on company-issued devices with known viewport.
Native app-like PWA with locked orientation.
Presentation/slide decks rendered at fixed aspect ratio.
detectorSignal: Fixed-width containers, pixel-based font and spacing with zero @media rules
matcherProposal: Parse CSS for @media rules. If none found AND CSS contains fixed-width declarations (width > 500px or max-width with px value) AND uses px font sizes, flag as potentially non-responsive. Also scan for viewport meta tag presence.
detectorLimitations: JavaScript-based responsive layouts (ResizeObserver, container queries) may not appear in static CSS analysis.
CSS Grid auto-fill/auto-fit can create responsive layouts without media queries.
Server-side responsive delivery may serve different CSS per device.
falsePositiveScenarios: A single-page app that handles responsiveness entirely through CSS Grid auto-fit with no media queries needed.
An email template where fixed-width is intentional for email client compatibility.
mitigationStrategy: Check for both CSS @media and JavaScript resize handlers. Check for viewport meta tag absence as additional signal. CSS Grid auto-fit without media queries is acceptable if content restacks correctly.

fixturePlans.violation: A landing page with width: 1200px, font-size: 18px, no @media rules, no viewport meta tag.
fixturePlans.control: A landing page with max-width: 1200px, width: 100%, font-size: clamp(16px, 1.125rem, 18px), three @media breakpoints, viewport meta tag.
fixturePlans.borderline: A dashboard using CSS Grid auto-fit with minmax(250px, 1fr) that restacks responsively without media queries.

cleanRoomAttestations: Standard responsive web design practice. WCAG 1.4.10 Reflow.
overlapNotes: Distinct from existing rules. No responsive-specific rule exists in core.json.
